notes:
Premise: Princess MC and her younger brother the prince are both secretly reincarnated, each hiding it (who admits that without sounding insane), each carrying a "book" memory of the world that doesn't quite match reality — and doesn't match each other's either.

The relationship: Siblings, not romance. Driven by mirrored, inverted prophecy: MC's book says the prince becomes a tyrant who destroys the house; the prince's book says the princess becomes the villainess who triggers the downfall. Each has spent the story quietly trying to save the other from a fate they're too scared to mention out loud.

The comedic engine: To stop a flag (e.g. house downfall),MC do A and prince do B from flawed memory, which resolves anyway as a side effect. Repeated with escalating stakes, this also fuels mutual suspicion, since secretive competence reads as "villain behavior" from the outside.

The reveal : Big crisis, the trick fails for real, panic forces a slip. Reveal happens in one tight burst: quick verification (a trivia-style "you're really from Earth too?" exchange, played for comedy) immediately followed by the real gut-punch — "wait, you did that because you thought I was the villain?" Recognition clears the animosity almost instantly; it was never personal, just fear. Auto-alliance, no extended will-they-trust-each-other arc.

Immediate pivot: Straight into investigating who or what is actually behind everything — since they obviously can't both secretly be "the villain," something else is generating the false prophecy, and their books contradicting each other is a clue, not just flavor.

Revised structure (~80 chapters):

ArcChaptersBeat1. Orientation1–8Setup, first A/B/C incident, prince introduced2. Parallel Vigilance9–20Secret meddling, mounting mutual suspicion3. Cracks21–32Tricks start failing → big crisis4. The Reveal33–36Fast verification + gut-punch + instant alliance5. The Investigation37–56No more reliable foreknowledge, real partnership, digging into who's behind the false prophecy and why the books don't match6. Convergence57–70True cause/antagonist exposed, stakes peak7. Resolution71–80Climax, resolution, epilogue Preferred interiority level: HIGH — regardless of grammatical POV (first or third).

What this means in practice:


The reader should get close to unfiltered access to a character's real-time thoughts: reactions, sarcasm, panic, half-formed reasoning — not a clean summary of what they felt after the fact.
Test sentence: does the prose ever just drop a raw thought straight into the narration, like dialogue ("What do we do?", "That was... not good.") — or does it always wrap thought in a reporting phrase ("she thought," "he felt")? The former = high interiority. The latter, used constantly, = low interiority, even in first person.


POV pronoun (first vs. third limited) is secondary to this.


Third person is perfectly fine if it still performs thought directly rather than narrating it from a remove.
